A History of Mind

Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
South Asian Traditions

Patañjali

c. 2nd c. BCE – 4th c. CE

Yoga as the stilling of citta: disciplined attention, witness-consciousness, and liberation from mental entanglement.

The images above are traditional iconographic depictions, not historical portraits. I will treat “Patañjali” here primarily as the figure associated with the Yoga Sūtras and the classical Pātañjala Yoga tradition, not as the grammarian of the Mahābhāṣya, although traditional accounts sometimes identify these figures with one another.

1. Central thesis

Patañjali’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a dualistic, soteriological, attentional, and therapeutic theory of consciousness. It is dualistic because it distinguishes puruṣa, pure witnessing consciousness, from prakṛti, nature or materiality, which includes not only the body but also the mind. It is soteriological because its ultimate concern is not explanation for its own sake but liberation: the disentangling of consciousness from misidentification with mental activity. It is attentional because the method of liberation is the progressive stabilization, refinement, and eventual cessation of mental modifications. It is therapeutic because suffering is traced to specific cognitive-affective distortions—ignorance, ego-identification, attachment, aversion, and fear of death—and yoga is presented as a disciplined means of uprooting them.

The famous opening definition is yogaś citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ: yoga is the restraint, cessation, or stilling of the modifications of citta, the mind-field. Patañjali immediately adds that when this happens, “the seer” abides in its own nature; otherwise it appears identified with the mind’s modifications. This is the compressed core of his philosophy of mind: ordinary consciousness is not pure awareness but awareness entangled with cognitive forms; liberation consists in discriminating awareness from those forms. Multiple translations preserve the same basic structure: yoga is the suppression, inhibition, cessation, restraint, or control of the mind’s modifications.

2. Historical position

Historically, Patañjali does not invent yoga, meditation, ascetic psychology, or Sāṅkhya metaphysics. His achievement is systematization. The Yoga Sūtras emerge from a larger Indian landscape that includes Upaniṣadic contemplative traditions, the Mahābhārata and Bhagavad Gītā, early ascetic disciplines, Buddhist and Jain meditation systems, and Sāṅkhya metaphysics. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy emphasizes that Patañjali’s yoga arose from a “cluster” of interconnected practices and that Sāṅkhya provides the metaphysical infrastructure for Yoga.

The date of the Yoga Sūtras remains uncertain. The IEP notes that most scholars date the text shortly after the turn of the Common Era, often around the first or second century CE, while also stating that it does not postdate the fifth century CE. It also notes that very little is known about Patañjali himself, and that later tradition sometimes identifies him with the grammarian Patañjali.

The text is a sūtra text: terse, mnemonic, and deliberately compressed. This matters because a philosophy of mind cannot responsibly be extracted from isolated aphorisms alone. The Yoga Sūtras are historically read with the early commentary attributed to Vyāsa, and the IEP stresses that understanding “Patañjali” usually means understanding Patañjali as interpreted by Vyāsa and the subsequent commentarial tradition.

In the wider history of philosophy of mind, Patañjali occupies a position analogous to, but independent from, several Western developments. Like Plato, he treats ordinary embodiment and sense-driven cognition as a source of confusion. Like Aristotle, he offers a systematic psychology of cognition and perception, though on very different metaphysical grounds. Like the Stoics, he treats mental training as ethically and existentially decisive. Like Descartes, he is a dualist, but not a Cartesian one: for Descartes, mind is the thinking substance; for Patañjali, ordinary mind is not the true conscious subject at all. It belongs to nature.

3. The metaphysical basis: puruṣa and prakṛti

Patañjali’s philosophy of mind is unintelligible without the Sāṅkhya distinction between puruṣa and prakṛti.

Puruṣa is pure witnessing consciousness: inactive, unchanging, contentless, and not itself a mental process. Prakṛti is nature, the matrix of material manifestation. Crucially, prakṛti includes not only gross matter but also subtle psychical structures: intellect, ego, mind, sense-capacities, memory traces, dispositions, and ordinary cognition. In Sāṅkhya-Yoga, therefore, “mind” is not the opposite of matter. Mind is subtle matter. The real dualism is not mind versus body, but consciousness versus nature.

This is one of Patañjali’s most important contributions to a comparative philosophy of mind. Western readers often expect a dualism of “mental” and “physical.” Patañjali instead gives us a three-level distinction:

First, there are external objects and bodily processes.

Second, there is the internal instrument: mind, intellect, ego, memory, attention, and sense-coordination.

Third, there is puruṣa, the witnessing consciousness in whose presence the mind appears conscious.

Thus the mind is not the final subject. It is an instrument. The mind processes, remembers, desires, imagines, misperceives, attends, and becomes absorbed. But it is not self-luminous in the ultimate sense. The Yoga Sūtras explicitly say that the mind is not self-luminous because it is itself knowable; it cannot be both perceiver and perceived at once; and it knows by reflecting both the seer and the seen.

This model has several consequences. Patañjali is not a reductive materialist, because consciousness is not reduced to body or mind. He is not an idealist, because objects are not merely projections of one mind; the same external object can be cognized differently by different minds. He is not a Cartesian dualist, because the thinking mind is not the ultimate self. He is closer to a witness-consciousness dualist: consciousness is irreducible, but thought belongs to nature.

4. The structure of mind: citta, buddhi, ahaṃkāra, manas

The central term in the Yoga Sūtras is citta, often translated as mind, mind-field, mind-stuff, or psychomental field. It is not identical with puruṣa. It is the mutable internal organ in which thoughts, perceptions, memories, dreams, habits, affective patterns, and meditative absorptions occur.

Classical Sāṅkhya-Yoga typically analyzes this internal organ through three principal functions:

Buddhi is intellect, discrimination, ascertainment, judgment, and the capacity for insight. It is the most sattvic, lucid aspect of the mind and the closest prakṛtic covering to puruṣa.

Ahaṃkāra is the “I-maker,” the ego-function. It appropriates experience as “I” and “mine,” giving cognition a first-person possessive structure.

Manas is the lower mind or sensory coordinator. It organizes sensory inputs, attends, doubts, and mediates between sense faculties and higher cognition.

The IEP’s treatment of Yoga and Sāṅkhya describes buddhi as the first evolute of prakṛti and the interface between puruṣa and the rest of nature; ahaṃkāra as the egoic function that processes reality from an individualized “I” and “mine”; and Sāṅkhya perception as a process in which mind constructs representations from sensory data while puruṣa supplies consciousness as witness.

This gives Patañjali a sophisticated layered psychology. Ordinary experience is not just “consciousness seeing the world.” It is a composite event involving sensory contact, internal representation, egoic appropriation, memory traces, affective coloring, and the illumination of these processes by puruṣa.

5. Mental modifications: the five vṛttis

Patañjali’s immediate taxonomy of mental life is the fivefold classification of vṛttis, or modifications of citta. These are:

Pramāṇa, valid cognition.

Viparyaya, error or false cognition.

Vikalpa, conceptual construction, imagination, or word-based cognition lacking a corresponding object.

Nidrā, sleep.

Smṛti, memory.

The Yoga Sūtras identify these five as the major forms of mental modification and then specify the valid means of knowledge as perception, inference, and reliable testimony.

This taxonomy is philosophically significant because even valid cognition is a vṛtti. Patañjali is not saying that all thought is false. Rather, he is saying that all thought, even true thought, is a modification of the mind. True cognition is useful, even indispensable, but it is not liberation. Liberation requires a further movement: not merely correcting false mental contents, but overcoming the identification of consciousness with mental content as such.

Pramāṇa gives Patañjali an epistemology. He accepts direct perception, inference, and authoritative testimony. Yet these are conventional means. They can orient the practitioner, but they remain limited because they operate through mental forms. In advanced samādhi, the tradition claims a higher form of direct yogic perception capable of grasping particulars beyond ordinary sensory range; this claim should be treated as a doctrinal feature of the system rather than as something established by ordinary philosophical argument.

Viparyaya is not merely factual error. Its deepest form is metaphysical error: taking the non-self to be the self. In Patañjali, ordinary egoic identity is not a harmless convention but the central cognitive distortion that sustains suffering.

Vikalpa is especially important for philosophy of language and mind. It is word-based cognition not grounded in a corresponding object. Patañjali thereby recognizes that language can generate meaningful mental content without objectual reference. This anticipates, in a very different vocabulary, later philosophical concerns about intentionality, reference, fiction, abstraction, and linguistic construction. The Yoga Sūtras also distinguish word, meaning, and object in the discussion of meditative insight.

Nidrā, sleep, is treated as a vṛtti, not as a mere absence of mind. The commentarial tradition argues that sleep must leave an impression, otherwise one could not later remember having slept well, badly, or in stupor. This is an important point: Patañjali’s psychology does not equate mind with waking conceptual thought. There are subliminal and non-waking mental states.

Smṛti, memory, is the retention or reactivation of past impressions. This leads into one of the most powerful parts of Patañjali’s psychology: the theory of latent traces.

6. Saṃskāra, memory, habit, and the proto-unconscious

A saṃskāra is a latent impression deposited in citta by experience, action, perception, and thought. Mental modifications leave traces; traces can later reactivate as further modifications; those modifications motivate action; action deposits further traces. Patañjali’s psychology is therefore dynamic and recursive.

The IEP describes memories as reactivations of saṃskāras and stresses that saṃskāras are not merely passive imprints. They can become active under conducive circumstances and shape thoughts, behaviors, traits, habits, compulsions, and addictions.

This is one of the strongest points of convergence with later Western depth psychology, though we should avoid claiming direct equivalence. Patañjali does not have Freud’s theory of repression, libido, symptom formation, or psychosexual development. But he does have a theory of latent psychical dispositions that operate beneath ordinary awareness and condition future perception, desire, and action. His account is also more ethically and soteriologically structured than modern clinical psychology: saṃskāras are not just psychological residues; they participate in karma, rebirth, suffering, and liberation.

The system is circular:

Experience produces vṛttis.

Vṛttis leave saṃskāras.

Saṃskāras reactivate as tendencies.

Tendencies shape perception, affect, and action.

Action produces further karmic residue.

This cycle is the psychological mechanism of bondage.

Patañjali’s solution is not simply introspective self-understanding. It is counter-conditioning and eventual cessation. Detrimental patterns are weakened by ethical discipline, meditative practice, dispassion, and contrary cultivation; sattvic patterns replace rājasic and tāmasic patterns; eventually even sattvic patterns must be transcended.

7. The afflictions: a theory of psychopathology and existential error

The five kleśas, or afflictions, are:

Avidyā, ignorance.

Asmitā, ego-identification.

Rāga, attachment.

Dveṣa, aversion.

Abhiniveśa, clinging to life or fear of death.

The Yoga Sūtras define these as the basic afflictions and describe ignorance as the field from which the others arise. Ignorance means taking the impermanent as permanent, the impure as pure, the painful as pleasurable, and the non-self as self. Egoism is the identification of the power of seeing with the instruments of seeing.

This is a deep theory of self-deception. Patañjali does not treat suffering as merely emotional pain. Suffering arises because consciousness is misidentified with the contents and instruments of cognition. The mind says “I see,” “I know,” “I desire,” “I fear,” “I remember,” and “I am this.” But for Patañjali, the true seer is not the mind, the ego, the body, or the stream of thought. It is puruṣa.

Attachment and aversion are memory-dependent. We remember pleasure and seek its repetition; we remember pain and seek its avoidance. The IEP describes rāga as craving for pleasure based on remembered pleasure, and dveṣa as resistance or resentment toward pain based on remembered pain. Thus memory is not neutral storage. It is affectively charged and motivationally active.

This makes Patañjali’s account of emotion cognitively rich. Desire and aversion are not brute drives; they are structured by prior cognition, memory, evaluation, and misidentification. The closest Western analogue is not Cartesian passion theory but something closer to Stoic and cognitive theories of emotion, where affect depends on judgment-like appraisal. The Stoics likewise held that rational agents are responsible for assent to impressions and that passions involve judgments or opinions; the convergence is striking, though the metaphysics differs sharply.

8. Perception and intentionality

In contemporary philosophy of mind, intentionality means the directedness of mental states: the fact that thoughts, perceptions, memories, and desires are about something. Brentano reintroduced intentionality into modern philosophy of psychology, and it remains central to philosophy of mind and phenomenology.

Patañjali does not use this vocabulary, but he clearly has an intentional theory of mind. Citta is “colored” by objects; the mind takes on forms corresponding to what is known; the same object can be cognized differently because different minds are differently conditioned. The Yoga Sūtras compare the stabilized mind to a transparent crystal capable of taking on the form of knower, knowing, or known.

This is a representational realism. Patañjali does not reduce the external object to a mental image. The object is real enough to be known by multiple minds, but cognition varies because the mind’s internal condition varies. The system therefore has three terms: object, mental representation, and witnessing consciousness.

This three-term model is one of Patañjali’s most distinctive contributions to philosophy of mind. It avoids naïve realism, because cognition is mediated by mind. It avoids subjective idealism, because objects are not merely private mental contents. It avoids reductive naturalism, because consciousness is not reduced to the psychophysical instrument.

9. Attention, concentration, meditation, and samādhi

Patañjali’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of attention. The mind is normally dispersed, colored, agitated, distracted, and conditioned by latent impressions. Yoga is the progressive transformation of attention from dispersion to one-pointedness to absorption to cessation.

The third chapter defines the inner limbs:

Dhāraṇā is fixing the mind in one place.

Dhyāna is the uninterrupted continuation of that mental flow.

Samādhi is the condition in which only the object shines forth, as if the mind’s own form disappears.

The three together are called saṃyama, disciplined integration or self-control, and Patañjali claims that mastery of saṃyama yields insight.

This is not merely relaxation or concentration in a casual sense. It is a graduated phenomenology of attention. At first, attention is effortful and mixed with conceptualization. Then it becomes continuous. Then the distinction between subject, act, and object becomes attenuated. Eventually even subtle meditative cognition is surpassed.

The first chapter distinguishes cognitive samādhi, accompanied by reasoning, subtle reflection, bliss, and “I-am-ness,” from a deeper state in which only latent impressions remain. It culminates in seedless samādhi, where even the impression born of higher knowledge is restrained.

In Western comparison, this is closer to a disciplined phenomenology than to armchair introspection. Phenomenology studies structures of consciousness from the first-person point of view, including perception, memory, imagination, emotion, desire, and volition. Patañjali is also first-person and structural, but with a crucial difference: phenomenology usually describes intentional consciousness, whereas Patañjali ultimately seeks the cessation of intentional modification.

10. Ethics as cognitive purification

The eight limbs of yoga are not merely moral rules plus meditation. They form a complete psychotechnical system for transforming citta:

Yama: restraints, including nonviolence, truthfulness, non-stealing, sexual restraint, and non-possessiveness.

Niyama: observances, including purity, contentment, austerity, study, and devotion to Īśvara.

Āsana: posture.

Prāṇāyāma: regulation of breath or vital energy.

Pratyāhāra: withdrawal or mastery of the senses.

Dhāraṇā: concentration.

Dhyāna: meditation.

Samādhi: absorption.

The Yoga Sūtras list these eight limbs, and the IEP emphasizes that ethical practices are essential for increasing sattva, the lucidity required for steadiness of mind.

This is a major difference from much modern philosophy of mind. Patañjali does not isolate cognition from character. The mind that lies, harms, grasps, indulges compulsive desire, and feeds envy is not merely morally defective; it is cognitively unfit for discriminative knowledge. Ethics purifies attention.

This resembles Stoicism in one respect: for both traditions, mental discipline and moral discipline are inseparable. But Patañjali’s metaphysical target is different. Stoicism seeks rational alignment with nature and disciplined assent to impressions; Patañjali seeks the disentangling of puruṣa from prakṛti.

11. Embodiment: posture, breath, senses

Patañjali is not a modern postural yoga theorist. The Yoga Sūtras devote very little space to āsana; posture is to be steady and comfortable, and its function is to support meditation. The IEP also notes that neither the Upaniṣads nor the Gītā treat āsana as modern stretching postures, and that Patañjali gives only three brief sūtras to posture.

Still, the body matters. Posture stabilizes the mind. Breath regulation removes coverings from the light and makes the mind fit for concentration. Sense-withdrawal prevents the mind from being constantly pulled outward by sensory objects.

This is not Cartesian interiority. The mind is not sealed off from the body. Patañjali’s psychology is embodied in practice, even though his metaphysics ultimately separates puruṣa from the psychophysical organism. Body, breath, senses, and mind are all prakṛtic processes; they can be disciplined together.

12. Īśvara and the role of devotion

Patañjali’s Yoga differs from classical non-theistic Sāṅkhya by admitting Īśvara, a special puruṣa untouched by afflictions, karma, impressions, and their fruits. Devotion to Īśvara, especially through repetition and contemplation of Oṃ, is presented as a powerful means of overcoming obstacles and attaining samādhi.

Philosophically, Īśvara is not needed to explain the existence of the world in the way a creator God is often invoked in Abrahamic metaphysics. Īśvara functions primarily as an unsurpassed meditative object, an archetype of unconditioned consciousness, and a practical aid to concentration. The IEP notes that Patañjali’s use of “or” in Yoga Sūtra 1.23 suggests devotion to Īśvara is presented as an optional route to samādhi in that context, though devotion also reappears in kriyā yoga and the niyamas.

For a philosophy of mind, Īśvara matters because Patañjali recognizes that attention can be stabilized not only by analytic discrimination but also by devotion, repetition, affective surrender, and symbolic concentration. The mind is not purified by cognition alone.

13. Liberation: kaivalya as the final state of mind’s non-identification

The final aim is kaivalya, isolation or aloneness: the complete disentangling of puruṣa from prakṛti. This should not be misunderstood as loneliness, nihilism, or dissociation. It means that consciousness no longer mistakes itself for the mind, body, ego, or world.

The Yoga Sūtras state that the seer is pure consciousness, that the seen exists for the sake of the seer, that ignorance causes their association, and that liberation comes through their dissociation by discriminative knowledge.

The final chapter presents a radical claim: when the distinction between mind and self is seen, confusion ceases; the mind inclines toward liberation; afflictions and karmic causality fall away; the guṇas have fulfilled their purpose; and the indweller shines forth as pure consciousness.

This is not the annihilation of consciousness. It is the cessation of the mind’s claim to be consciousness. Nor is it ordinary self-knowledge. It is not “I now understand myself better.” It is the collapse of the mistaken identification between “I” and the psychomental apparatus.

14. Comparison with Indian Buddhist philosophy of mind

Patañjali and Buddhism share a great deal: meditation, attention training, analysis of afflictions, concern with suffering, latent dispositions, and critique of ordinary egoity. Both traditions see ordinary self-experience as constructed and delusive. Both treat disciplined attention as central to liberation. Both understand mental life dynamically rather than as a static substance.

The decisive difference is the self. Buddhist philosophy centers on anātman, not-self: the rejection of a permanent self as the owner or agent of sensory and mental activity. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes Buddhist accounts as challenging the Upaniṣadic idea of an enduring substantive ātman and analyzing the person as a composite of physical and psychological elements.

Patañjali agrees that body, ego, thought, memory, and personality are not the true self. But he does not conclude that there is no enduring witnessing principle. He posits puruṣa. Thus, from a Buddhist perspective, Patañjali retains a subtle self. From Patañjali’s perspective, Buddhism risks failing to distinguish the witnessing consciousness from the stream of mental events.

There are also technical convergences. Buddhist Abhidharma analyzes mind through citta and mental constituents, treats attention as crucial to cognition, and develops theories of mind-stream, seeds, and repository consciousness in later traditions. These are structurally comparable to Patañjali’s citta, saṃskāra, and vāsanā-like patterns, though the metaphysical commitments differ.

15. Comparison with Sāṅkhya

Patañjali’s Yoga is best understood as practical Sāṅkhya with a stronger meditative and theistic orientation. Sāṅkhya supplies the metaphysical distinction between puruṣa and prakṛti, the doctrine of the guṇas, and the analysis of mind as part of nature. Yoga supplies the disciplined method by which discriminative knowledge becomes existentially transformative. The IEP explicitly says Sāṅkhya gives Yoga its metaphysical basis, while Yoga provides the technique or practice.

Sāṅkhya is often described as atheistic or non-theistic in its classical form; Yoga admits Īśvara. But in philosophy of mind, their core agreement is more important: consciousness is not a product of the mind; mind is an instrument illuminated by consciousness.

16. Comparison with Plato

Plato and Patañjali converge in treating ordinary sense-bound life as cognitively unstable and spiritually dangerous. In Plato’s Phaedo, the soul is associated with intelligible reality and becomes confused when it attends to perceptible things; wisdom involves turning away from bodily distraction toward a purer object of knowledge.

But Plato’s soul is not Patañjali’s puruṣa. Plato’s soul reasons, rules, learns, desires, and can be virtuous or vicious. Patañjali’s puruṣa does none of these. Reasoning, virtue, desire, confusion, and meditative refinement all belong to citta, which is prakṛti. The Platonic soul is still psychologically active. Puruṣa is not.

This is the key contrast: Plato spiritualizes reason; Patañjali de-psychologizes consciousness.

17. Comparison with Aristotle

Aristotle’s psychology is hylomorphic. Soul is the form or actuality of a living body; psychology studies the soul as the principle of life, including plant, animal, and human functions. Aristotle’s approach therefore does not begin with a sharp separation between consciousness and nature. The soul-body relation is a special case of form-matter relation, and Aristotle’s hylomorphism is neither reductive materialism nor Platonic separability.

Patañjali is almost the inverse. He agrees that ordinary psychophysical functions are natural, but he refuses to identify the true conscious subject with those functions. In Aristotelian terms, buddhi, manas, ego, perception, affect, and memory might look like psychic functions of an organism. For Patañjali, they are still not the self.

Aristotle gives a philosophy of ensouled life. Patañjali gives a philosophy of how consciousness becomes misidentified with ensouled life.

18. Comparison with Stoicism

Stoicism and Patañjali converge as therapeutic philosophies. Both regard disciplined attention as central. Both see ordinary suffering as bound up with mistaken evaluation. Both think freedom depends on a trained response to impressions.

The Stoics, however, place the decisive emphasis on assent: rational agents are not responsible for receiving impressions but are responsible for assenting or withholding assent. The SEP notes that assent allows rational agents to adopt a critical stance toward impressions and that moral and epistemic character depends on acts of assent.

Patañjali’s practice is more radical. He does not merely train assent to impressions; he seeks cessation of the vṛttis that constitute ordinary mental life. Stoicism disciplines judgment within the rational mind. Patañjali disciplines the mind until consciousness is no longer identified with mind at all.

19. Comparison with Descartes

The comparison with Descartes is essential because both are dualists, yet their dualisms are fundamentally different.

Descartes distinguishes mind and body as substances: mind is characterized by thought, body by extension. This Cartesian dualism became a standard starting point for the modern mind-body problem.

Patañjali does not identify the self with thinking substance. Thought is a vṛtti of citta. Citta is part of prakṛti. The true seer is not the thinker but the witness of thinking. Therefore, the Cartesian cogito—“I think”—would not be final for Patañjali. It would still be a subtle form of asmitā, the identification of the power of seeing with the instrument of seeing.

The difference can be expressed sharply:

Descartes: I think, therefore I am.

Patañjali: Thinking occurs in citta; the seer is confused when it identifies with that thinking.

This makes Patañjali a powerful alternative to the dominant Western framing of the mind-body problem. His problem is not primarily how immaterial mind interacts with extended body. His problem is how pure consciousness appears bound to the psychomental machinery of nature.

20. Comparison with Hume

Hume’s bundle theory denies a substantial self behind the stream of perceptions. The self is, in this view, nothing over and above a changing bundle of experiences or perceptions.

Patañjali partially agrees. The empirical ego is not a true self. Personality, memory, desire, and identity are constructed within citta. If Hume is attacking the idea that introspection reveals a stable ego-object among perceptions, Patañjali would agree.

But Patañjali rejects the conclusion that there is no enduring witnessing consciousness. Hume finds no self beyond the bundle; Patañjali says the very knowability of the bundle presupposes the seer. Hume dissolves self into perceptions. Patañjali distinguishes puruṣa from all perceptual and mental content.

21. Comparison with Kant

Kant is a closer comparison than Descartes in one respect: he distinguishes the empirical self from a formal condition of experience. Kant’s transcendental apperception is not an object discovered in inner sense; it is the unity required for representations to belong together as one experience. The SEP describes Kant’s model as centered on synthesis, unity of consciousness, and the mind’s functions in organizing experience.

Patañjali’s puruṣa is not Kant’s transcendental unity of apperception. Kant’s “I think” is a condition of unified cognition; Patañjali’s puruṣa is a metaphysical witness beyond cognition. Kant does not claim that the transcendental subject can be isolated in seedless samādhi. Patañjali does.

Still, the comparison is fruitful. Both distinguish the empirical ego from a deeper condition of experience. Both deny that the true subject is simply another object among objects. But Kant remains within the architecture of cognition; Patañjali aims to go beyond cognition’s modifications.

22. Comparison with Brentano, James, and phenomenology

Brentano reintroduced intentionality into modern philosophy as a defining feature of mental phenomena. Modern phenomenology then developed first-person analysis of conscious experience, intentionality, temporality, embodiment, and self-awareness.

Patañjali is deeply relevant here because he offers a refined first-person taxonomy of mental states, attention, memory, language-based construction, sleep, absorption, and self-misidentification. But his project is not merely descriptive. Phenomenology often brackets metaphysical questions in order to describe consciousness. Patañjali brackets ordinary mental activity in order to reveal consciousness as distinct from it.

William James famously described the “stream of thought” and helped establish psychology as a philosophical and scientific inquiry into lived mental flow. Patañjali’s vṛtti theory also analyzes the stream, but his central claim is that awareness need not remain identical with the stream. The Jamesian stream is the field to be studied; the Patañjalian stream is the field to be stilled.

23. Patañjali and contemporary philosophy of mind

Patañjali speaks directly to several contemporary debates, though not in contemporary terms.

On the hard problem of consciousness, Patañjali would reject the assumption that consciousness is to be derived from physical or functional processes. Consciousness is puruṣa: irreducible, contentless, and not produced by citta. This is not a scientific solution to the hard problem; it is a metaphysical refusal of physicalist reduction.

On higher-order theories, Patañjali would distinguish awareness from metacognition. A thought about a thought is still a vṛtti. The witness is not a higher-order representation inside the mind.

On self-model theories, he would agree that the empirical self is constructed, but he would deny that this exhausts subjectivity. Ahaṃkāra is a self-model; puruṣa is not.

On cognitive science of attention, he offers a sophisticated practical taxonomy: distraction, one-pointedness, sensory withdrawal, sustained attention, absorption, and cessation. His account is not experimentally framed, but it is phenomenologically precise.

On unconscious processing, saṃskāra theory provides an ancient model of latent dispositions shaping cognition and behavior. It is not identical to modern implicit cognition or psychoanalysis, but it clearly recognizes that mental life is not exhausted by occurrent conscious thought.

On embodied cognition, Patañjali’s practice shows that posture, breath, senses, diet, ethical conduct, and attention are interdependent. His metaphysics is not embodied in the contemporary naturalist sense, but his method is strongly embodied.

24. The extracted Patañjalian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Patañjalian philosophy of mind can be formulated as the following theses.

1. Consciousness is not a mental state. Puruṣa is not perception, thought, memory, pleasure, pain, or ego. It is the witnessing condition under which these become manifest.

2. Mind is subtle nature. Citta is not pure consciousness. It is a mutable, conditioned, guṇa-structured instrument belonging to prakṛti.

3. Ordinary experience is a composite. Perception involves object, sense, mind, ego, intellect, memory, affective coloring, and witnessing consciousness.

4. Mental states are intentional modifications. Vṛttis are forms the mind takes with respect to objects, words, memories, absences, and imagined constructs.

5. Valid cognition is not liberation. True cognition is preferable to error, but even true cognition is still a modification of citta.

6. The empirical self is constructed. Ahaṃkāra appropriates experience as “I” and “mine.” This is necessary for ordinary functioning but metaphysically mistaken when taken as the true self.

7. Suffering is cognitive-affective misidentification. The kleśas arise from avidyā, especially taking the non-self to be self.

8. The mind is historically conditioned. Saṃskāras and memory traces shape future perception, desire, fear, personality, and action.

9. Attention is transformable. Practice can move the mind from dispersion to stability, from stability to absorption, and from absorption to cessation.

10. Ethics is mental technology. Nonviolence, truthfulness, restraint, contentment, study, and devotion are not merely moral ornaments; they alter the structure and clarity of citta.

11. Liberation is discriminative non-identification. Kaivalya is achieved when puruṣa is no longer confused with citta.

12. The final state is not unconsciousness. Nirodha is not blank annihilation but the cessation of mental modifications such that pure witnessing consciousness stands alone.

25. Philosophical strengths

Patañjali’s system has several enduring strengths.

It sharply distinguishes consciousness from mental content. This remains philosophically powerful because many confusions in philosophy of mind arise from treating awareness, thought, self-representation, and personality as one thing.

It provides a deep account of habitual conditioning. The saṃskāra model recognizes that perception and desire are historically shaped; the mind is not a transparent mirror unless disciplined.

It integrates ethics, cognition, affect, and attention. Modern philosophy often separates these domains. Patañjali sees them as one system.

It gives an unusually detailed model of attention training. Dhāraṇā, dhyāna, samādhi, saṃyama, and nirodha form a graded theory of attentional transformation.

It offers a sophisticated critique of egoic selfhood without collapsing into simple no-self reductionism. The empirical ego is false as ultimate identity, but subjectivity is not denied.

26. Philosophical difficulties

The system also faces serious philosophical challenges.

The first is the interaction or relation problem. If puruṣa is inactive and prakṛti is unconscious, how exactly does experience occur? Sāṅkhya-Yoga often appeals to proximity or reflection metaphors, but these can look explanatory rather than fully explanatory. The IEP notes that later Sāṅkhya develops a reflection theory to address how unchanging puruṣa and material psyche relate.

The second is the multiplicity of puruṣas. If puruṣas are qualityless, inactive, and contentless, what distinguishes one puruṣa from another? Sāṅkhya offers arguments from the diversity and privacy of experience, but the problem remains philosophically delicate.

The third is the epistemic status of pure consciousness. Patañjali claims that meditative practice reveals puruṣa, but from an external philosophical standpoint this raises questions: is contentless awareness knowable? If it is known, is it not an object? If it is not an object, how can it be established?

The fourth is the status of extraordinary powers. The third chapter discusses supernormal capacities arising from saṃyama, while also warning that such powers can become obstacles. A modern reconstruction can treat these as part of the tradition’s internal phenomenology and metaphysics, but they are not independently established by the philosophical argument alone.

The fifth is the possible tension between world-disengagement and ethical life. Patañjali’s system strongly values nonviolence, truth, compassion, and equanimity, but its final ideal is isolation of puruṣa from prakṛti. Whether this produces a coherent social philosophy is a further question.

27. Final synthesis

Patañjali’s philosophy of mind is one of the most rigorous premodern analyses of consciousness, cognition, attention, memory, egoity, affect, and liberation. Its central originality lies in refusing to identify consciousness with mind. The mind is active, mutable, representational, conditioned, ethical or unethical, pure or impure, distracted or absorbed. Consciousness is none of these. Consciousness is the seer.

Compared with Western developments, Patañjali is closest to a synthesis of Platonic purification, Stoic therapy of impressions, phenomenological first-person discipline, and anti-Cartesian dualism. Yet he is reducible to none of them. His most radical claim is that thinking is not the essence of the self. The self, if that term is used for puruṣa, is what remains when thought no longer misappropriates consciousness.

In that sense, the Yoga Sūtras are not merely a manual of meditation. They are a compressed and highly disciplined philosophy of mind whose guiding question is: What is awareness when it is no longer confused with the movements of mind?

PreviousĪśvarakṛṣṇa NextNāgārjuna

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
